content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 21, 2014

Roger C. Peters
BEST BEST & KRIEGER
2001 N Main Street, Suite 390
Walnut Creek, CA 94596

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. C-14-036

Dear Mr. Peters:

This letter responds to your request on behalf of Colma Mayor Helen Fisicaro for advice regarding the conflicts-of-interest provisions under Government Code Section 1090 et seq.¹ Because the Fair Political Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), this letter is based on the facts presented.

Please note that after forwarding your request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Mateo District Attorney's Office, we did not receive a written response from either entity. (See Section 1097.1(c)(4).) Finally, we are required to advise you that the advice given in this letter is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor. (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTION

Does Section 1090 prevent the Town of Colma city council from entering into a contract with PG&E, a company in which Mayor Fisicaro may have financial interests?

CONCLUSION

No. While Mayor Fisicaro does have a remote interest in PG&E under Section 1090, the City Council is not prohibited from entering the contract, provided that Mayor Fisicaro does not participate and follows the correct procedures applicable to disqualification when an official has a remote interest.

_______________
¹ All further statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. C-14-036
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are the city attorney for the Town of Colma, a general law city located in San Mateo
County and a customer of PG&E for its electric and gas services. Mayor Helen Fisicaro is a
retired employee of PG&E as of February 1, 2010. As a former employee, Mayor Fisicaro has a
401(k) plan comprised entirely of PG&E Corporation stock in an amount in excess of $2,000.
The Mayor's 401(k) plan holds less than three percent of the outstanding shares of PG&E
Corporation common stock and the dividends exceed five percent of her annual income. The
dividends paid on the Mayor's stock to her 401(k) plan are not paid to her as income. Rather,
they are reinvested into more PG&E Corporation stock in the 401(k) plan. The dividends are not
treated as income to her for tax purposes.

In addition to the 401(k) plan, the Mayor also receives payments from an Internal
Revenue Code Section 401(a) qualified, defined benefit pension plan that was established
through her previous employment at PG&E. As a defined benefit plan, the pension benefits are
guaranteed regardless of the performance of the underlying assets, and the nature of those
underlying assets is immaterial to the Plan's beneficiaries.

The City Council of the Town of Colma is contemplating entering into two agreements
with PG&E. Mayor Fisicaro intends to disclose her interests in her 401(k) plan and her pension
plan, and will disqualify herself from participating in making either agreement. The first
agreement is a loan agreement funded by California utility customers and administered by PG&E
under the auspices of the California Public Utilities Commission (CPUC). The program is
entitled the Energy Efficiency Retrofit Loan Program, also known as On-Bill Financing
("OBF"). The OBF loan program provides qualified PG&E customers with a means to finance
energy-efficiency upgrades. Any loan issued under the program is an interest-free, unsecured
loan intended to fully reimburse qualified PG&E customers for the costs incurred in connection
with energy efficiency upgrades. In essence, PG&E loans funds to a qualified customer to pay
the costs associated with energy efficiency upgrades. The loan is then repaid monthly through
the savings realized from the energy efficiency upgrades. PG&E states that the utility makes no
profit from this program, as evidenced by the zero percent interest rate on the loan.

The second agreement would be for PG&E to replace street lighting in the Town with
energy efficient light emitting diode (LED) lights. Funds from the OBF loan agreement would
be used to pay PG&E to replace the existing street lighting with energy-efficient LED lights.
PG&E would do all work involved, including purchasing LED lights, actually replacing the
street lighting in the Town, and arranging for disposal of the removed street lights. PG&E sets a
10 percent gross margin (herein, "administrative cost") on all streetlight replacement work,
which is then used to keep the program running. Any funds remaining after paying the
administrative cost are allocated roughly equally to the shareholders and ratepayers.

The work in Colma is estimated to cost around $44,854. PG&E states that any money
remaining after costs would be distributed among ratepayers and shareholders. You estimate
Mayor Fisicaro would receive approximately $0.19 (total), based on her holdings.

File No. C-14-036
Page No. 3

ANALYSIS

Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended "not only to strike at actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." (City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.)

Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest." (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 23 Cal.3d 633, 646.) When an officer with a proscribed financial interest is a member of the governing body of a public entity, the prohibition of section 1090 also extends to the entire body. (89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. at 50.) The prohibition applies regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Thomson, supra, at pp. 646-649.)

We employ the following six-step analysis to determine whether Mayor Fisicaro has a disqualifying conflict of interest under Section 1090.

Step One: Is the Mayor subject to the provisions of Section 1090?

Section 1090 provides, in part, that "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district, judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members." City councils and their members are plainly covered by this prohibition. (See, e.g., Thomson, supra, at p. 645; City Council v. McKinley (1978) 80 Cal.App.3d 204, 213.)

Step Two: Does the decision at issue involve a contract?

To determine whether a contract is involved in the decision, one may look to general principles of contract law (84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36 (2001);³ 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230, 234 (1995)), while keeping in mind that "specific rules applicable to Sections 1090 and 1097 require that we view the transactions in a broad manner and avoid narrow and technical definitions of 'contract.'" (People v. Honig, supra, at p. 351 citing Stigall, supra, at pp. 569, 571.)

Here, the Town of Colma will be providing consideration to PG&E in exchange for services. The parties are entering into a contract.

³ It is noteworthy to point out that opinions issued by the Attorney General's Office are entitled to considerable weight (California Assn. of Psychology Providers v. Rank (1990) 51 Cal.3d 1, 17), especially where, as here, it has regularly provided advice concerning a particular area of law. (Thorpe v. Long Beach Community College Dist., (2000) 83 Cal.App.4th 655, 662; Freedom Newspapers, Inc. v. Orange County Employees Retirement System (1993) 6 Cal.4th 821, 829.)

File No. C-14-036
Page No. 4

Step Three: Is the Mayor making or participating in making a contract?

As a member of the Town of Colma City Council, which must approve the contracts with
PG&E, Mayor Fisicaro would be participating in making of a contract.

Step Four: Does the Mayor have a financial interest in the contract?

Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official
has a financial interest" (People v. Honig, supra, at p. 333), and officials are deemed to have a
financial interest in a contract if they might profit from it in any way. (Ibid.) Although Section
1090 nowhere specifically defines the term "financial interest," case law and Attorney General
Opinions state that prohibited financial interests may be indirect as well as direct, and may
involve financial losses, or the possibility of losses, as well as the prospect of pecuniary gain.
(See e.g., Thomson, supra, at pp. 645, 651-652; see also People v. Vallerga (1977) 67
Cal.App.3d 847, 867, fn. 5.)

You have presented four possible financial interests, which we discuss in turn.

First, Mayor Fisicaro has stock holdings in PG&E valued in excess of $2,000 by virtue of
her 401(k) plan. Through that plan, she owns less than three percent of the total shares offered
by PG&E. Second, Mayor Fisicaro is paid dividends on these stocks, which are automatically
reinvested into her 401(k) account. The dividend amounts exceed five percent of the Mayor's
annual income. You have estimated that one of the contracts between PG&E and the city could
increase her dividend potential by approximately $0.19 (total).

Although, as mentioned, Section 1090 does not define the term "financial interest," other
provisions related to Section 1090 offer guidance on this issue as it relates to stock holdings and
dividends paid on those stocks. For example, the Legislature has created various statutory
exceptions to Section 1090's prohibition when the financial interest involved is deemed to be a
"remote interest," as defined in Section 1091, or a "noninterest," as defined in Section 1091.5.
Section 1091.5(a)(1) provides that an official is not deemed to be financially interested in a
contract made with a for-profit corporation when he or she owns less than three percent of the
shares of the corporation, provided that the total annual income to the official from dividends of
the stock does not exceed five percent of the official's total annual income and any other
payments made to the official by the corporation do not exceed five percent of the official's total
annual income. In addition, Section 1091(b)(14) provides that an official has a remote interest in
a public agency's contract with a for-profit corporation when he or she owns less than three
percent of the shares in the corporation and the stock ownership is derived from the official's
employment with the corporation. It therefore appears that the Legislature considers stock
ownership in a corporation with whom a public agency is contracting to be a type of financial
interest that raises issues under Section 1090.

Third and fourth, Mayor Fisicaro, as a former employee of PG&E, has an interest in a
401(a) defined-benefit pension plan offered by PG&E and, as a retiree, receives payments from

File No. C-14-036
Page No. 5

that plan. As a defined-benefit plan, the pension benefits are guaranteed regardless of the
performance of the underlying assets.

Section 1090 offers no guidance on whether an interest in a defined-benefit pension plan
and fixed payments under that plan are a type of financial interest. Therefore, we look to other
sources for assistance. First, in determining a Section 1090 financial interest, courts "generally
focus on whether the contract in question could confer some type of pecuniary advantage to the
target of a Section 1090 inquiry." (See Eden Township Healthcare District v. Sutter Health
(2011) 202 Cal. App. 4th 208, 225.) In Eden Township, the Court concluded that an employee
who received a salary from a contracting party, but whose salary or benefits would be in no way
affected by the contract, did not have a financial interest in the contract. (Id. at pp. 221-222 and
p. 228 ["In our view, if the contract itself offers no benefit to the official, either directly or
indirectly, then the official is not financially interested in the contract. . . ."].) Similarly, the
Mayor's 401(a) plan and payments made under the plan, which are defined, fixed benefits, will
not be, and indeed cannot be by operation of law, affected by any contract between PG&E and
the City of Colma. Based on this analysis, we conclude that, under these facts, the defined
benefit under the 401(a) plan (and payments made under the plan) is not a financial interest for
purposes of Section 1090.

The Political Reform Act (the "Act")³ is also instructive in defining financial interests for
purposes of Section 1090. We are guided by Lexin v. Superior Court (2010) 47 Cal.4th 1050,
1090-91, which applies the "in pari materia" canon of statutory construction in determining that
Section 1090 should be harmonized with the Act when possible. As explained by the court, "it is
well established that Section 1090 and the Act are "in pari materia." (Ibid.) Under the Act,
public officials are prohibited from participating in government decisions in which they have a
financial interest. (Section 87100.) Among other things, an official is considered to have a
financial interest in his or her source of income. (Section 87103(c).) The Act specifically
excludes from the definition of "income," however, "[p]ayments received under a defined benefit
pension plan qualified under Internal Revenue Code Section 401(a)." On this basis, the
Commission has advised that an official likewise has no separate investment interest under the
Act in the defined-benefit pension plan itself. (Bogh Advice Letter, No. A-01-173.) Thus, for
purposes of the Act's conflict of interest provisions, Mayor Fisicaro³ has no financial interest in
either the 401(a) pension plan itself or payments received under the plan. We reach the same
conclusion under Section 1090.

Step Five: Does either a remote interest or non-interest exception apply?

Based on the discussion above, we have concluded that Mayor Fisicaro's only financial
interest in PG&E for purposes of Section 1090's application to the proposed contracts is her
stock ownership under the 401(k) plan and the related dividends.

³ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. C-14-036
Page No. 6

As a general rule, when Section 1090 is applicable to one member of a governing body of
a public entity, as here, the prohibition cannot be avoided by having the interested board member
abstain; the entire governing body is precluded from entering into the contract. (Thomson,
supra, at pp. 647-649; Stigall, supra, at p. 569; 86 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 138, 139 (2003); 70
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 45, 48 (1987).) As mentioned, however, the Legislature has created various
statutory exceptions to Section 1090's prohibition, one of which defines remote interests under
Section 1091.

Non-Interest Exception

As discussed above, an official is not deemed to be financially interested in a contract
made with a for-profit corporation when he or she owns less than three percent of the shares of
the corporation, provided that the total annual income to the official from dividends of the stock
does not exceed five percent of the official's total annual income and any other payments made
to the official by the corporation do not exceed five percent of the official's total annual income.
(Section 1091.5(a)(1).) In Mayor Fisicaró's case, she owns less than three percent of PG&E's
stock, but receives more than five percent of her total annual income from the dividends. It can
be argued that, because her stock dividends are not paid directly to her as income but are instead
used by PG&E to purchase more stock on her behalf, that the dividends are technically not part
of her annual income, and thus her stock interest (and dividends) are a noninterest under Section
1091.5(a)(1).¹ We do not, however, view the concept of "income" under Section 1090 to rest
upon technical distinctions, such as, for example, that the payment of these dividends may be
treated differently under tax law than direct dividend payments made to an official. Here, the
payments are made to the benefit of Mayor Fisicaró through the plan, and the only difference is
the form in which she receives them. On this basis, we cannot conclude that Mayor Fisicaró's
stock ownership in PG&E (and the dividends) are a noninterest under Section 1091.5(a)(1).

Remote Interest Exception

If a "remote interest" is present, the contract may be made if (1) the officer in question
discloses his or her financial interest in the contract to the public agency, (2) such interest is
noted in the entity's official records, and (3) the officer abstains from any participation in the
making of the contract. (Section 1091(a); 88 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 106, 108 (2005); 83
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 246, 248 (2000).)

As stated above, a remote interest includes "a person who owns less than three percent of
the shares of a contracting party that is a for-profit corporation, provided that the ownership of
the shares derived from the person's employment with that corporation." (Section 1091(b)(14).)
The PG&E stock held in Mayor Fisicaró's 401(k) account fits precisely under this exception.
Moreover, we presume that the Legislature, in creating this exception, recognized that dividends
are commonly paid on stock shares held in a corporation and that these were not by themselves a
separate financial interest for purposes of this exception. Accordingly, we conclude that the

¹ If a "noninterest" is present, the contract may be made without the officer's abstention, and generally a
noninterest does not require disclosure. (City of Vernon v. Central Basin Mun. Water Dist. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th
508, 514-515; 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 159-160 (2001).)

File No. C-14-036
Page No. 7

401(k) account (and dividends paid on the stock in the account) is a remote interest for purposes
of Section 1090.

Mayor Fisicaro therefore has a remote interest in her 401(k) plan. The city council may
enter into the contract with PG&E, but the Mayor may not participate, as explained above.³

Step Six: Does the rule of necessity apply?

In limited circumstances, a "rule of necessity" has been applied to allow the making of a
contract that Section 1090 would otherwise prohibit. (88 Ops.Cal.Att.Gen. 106, 110 (2005).)
Under the rule of necessity, a government agency may acquire an essential service, despite the
existence of a conflict, when no source other than that which triggers the conflict is available;
the rule "ensures that essential government functions are performed even where a conflict of
interest exists." (Eldridge v. Sierra View Hospital Dist. (1990) 224 Cal. App. 3d 311, 322.)

You have provided no facts to suggest the "rule of necessity" would apply in the present
situation.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Zackery P. Morazzini
General Counsel

[signature: Heather M Rowan]

By: Heather M. Rowan
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

HMR:jgl

³ Because we conclude that Mayor Fisicaro has a remote interest regarding her stock interest in PG&E, we
do not address the issue of whether the noninterest exception of Section 1091.5(a)(3), concerning "public services
generally provided," applies to either or both of the contracts in issue. We note generally that this provision only
applies to the financial interest, if any, an official might have as a recipient of public services under a contract with a
public agency. It does not address another type of financial interest the official may have in the contract, such as
Mayor Fisicaro's interests in PG&E. As to whether or not the Mayor has a separate financial interest by virtue of
being a potential recipient of public benefits under these contracts, we discern none in the contract for replacement
of current street lights with LED lights and none at this stage in the contract to formulate the loan program. If, after
formulation of the loan program Mayor Fisicaro desires to apply for a loan, we would be pleased to provide an
analysis of whether the exception in Section 1091.5(a)(3) applies to her loan contract.

sections.question:
Does Section 1090 prevent the Town of Colma city council from entering into a contract 
with PG&E, a company in which Mayor Fisicaro may have financial interests?
sections.conclusion:
No. While Mayor Fisicaro does have a remote interest in PG&E under Section 1090, the 
City Council is not prohibited from entering the contract, provided that Mayor Fisicaro does not 
participate and follows the correct procedures applicable to disqualification when an official has 
a remote interest. 
1 All further statutory references arc to the· Government Code, unless otherwise indicated.
sections.facts:
File No. C-14-036  
You are the city attorney for the Town of Colma, a general law city located in San Mateo 
County and a customer of PG&E for its electric and gas services. Mayor Helen Fisicaro is a 
retired employee of PG&E as of February 1, 20 l 0. As a former employee, Mayor Fisicaro has a 
401 (k) plan comprised entirely of PG&E Corporation stock in an amount in excess of $2,000. 
The Mayor's 40l(k) plan holds less than three percent of the outstanding shares of PG&E 
Corporation common stock and the dividends exceed five percent of her annual income. The 
dividends paid on the Mayor's stock to her 401(k) plan are not paid to her as income. Rather, 
they are reinvested into more PG&E Corporation stock in the 40l(k) plan. The dividends are not 
treated as income to her for tax purposes. 
In addition to the 401 (k) plan, the Mayor also receives payments from an Internal 
Revenue Code Section 401(a) qualified, defined benefit pension plan that was established 
through her previous employment at PG&E. As a defined benefit plan, the pension benefits are 
guaranteed regardless of the performance of the underlying assets, and the nature of those 
underlying assets is immaterial to the Plan's beneficiaries. 
The City Council of the Town of Colma is contemplating entering into two agreements 
with PG&E. Mayor Fisicaro intends to disclose her interests in her 40 l (k) plan and her pension 
plan, and will disqualify herself from participating in making either agreement. The first 
agreement is a loan agreement funded by California utility customers and administered by PG&E 
under the auspices of the California Public Utilities Commission (CPUC). The program is 
entitled the Energy Efficiency Retrofit Loan Program, also known as On-Bill Financing 
("OBF"). The OBF loan program provides qualified PG&E customers with a means to finance 
energy-efficiency upgrades. Any loan issued under the program is an interest-free, unsecured 
loan intended to fully reimburse qualified PG&E customers for the costs incurred in connection 
with energy efficiency upgrades. In essence, PG&E loans funds to a qualified customer to pay 
the costs associated with energy efficiency upgrades. The loan is then repaid monthly through 
the savings realized from the energy efficiency upgrades. PG&E states that the utility makes no 
profit from this program, as evidenced by the zero percent interest rate on the loan. 
The second agreement would be for PG&E to replace street lighting in the Town with 
energy efficient light emitting diode (LED) lights. Funds from the OBF loan agreement would 
be used to pay PG&E to replace the existing street lighting with energy-efficient LED lights. 
PG&E would do all work involved, including purchasing LED lights, actually replacing the 
street lighting in the Town, and arranging for disposal of the removed street lights. PG&E sets a 
IO percent gross margin (herein, "administrative cost") on all streetlight replacement work, 
which is then used to keep the program running. Any funds remaining after paying the 
administrative cost are allocated roughly equally to the shareholders and ratepayers. 
The work in Colma is estimated to cost around $44,854. PG&E states that any money 
remaining after costs would be distributed among ratepayers and shareholders. You estimate 
Mayor Fisicaro would receive approximately $0.19 (total), based on her holdings.

File No. C-14-036  
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section I 090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their 
agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended "not only to 
strike at actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." (City of 
Imperial Beach v. Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official 
has a financial interest." (People v. Honig (l 996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that 
violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) When an officer 
with a proscribed financial interest is a member of the governing body of a public entity, the 
prohibition of section 1090 also extends to the entire body. (89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. at 50.) The 
prohibition applies regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all 
parties. (Thomson, supra, at pp. 646-649.) 
We employ the following six-step analysis to determine whether Mayor Fisicaro has a 
disqualifying conflict of interest under Section l 090. 
Step One: Is the Mayor subject to the provisions of Section 1090? 
Section 1090 provides, in part, that "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district, 
judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract 
made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members." 
City councils and their members are plainly covered by this prohibition. (See, e.g., Thomson, 
supra, at p. 645; City Council v. McKinley ( 1978) 80 Cal.App.3d 204, 213.) 
Step Two: Does the decision at issue involve a contract? 
To determine whether a contract is involved in the decision, one may look to general 
principles of contract law (84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36 (2001);? 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230,234 
(1995)), while keeping in mind that "specific rules applicable to Sections 1090 and 1097 require 
that we view the transactions in a broad manner and avoid narrow and technical definitions of 
•contract."' (People v. Honig, supra, at p. 351 citing Stigall, supra, at pp. 569, 571.) 
Here, the Town of Colma will be providing consideration to PG&E in exchange for 
services. The parties are entering into a contract.

considerable weight ( California Ass11. of Psychology Pro1•iders 11. Rank ( 1990) 51 Cal.3d 1, 17), especially where, as 
here, it has regularly provided advice concerning a particular area of law. (Thorpe 1•. Long Beach Com1111111ity 
College Dist., (2000) 83 Cal.App.4th 655, 662; Freedom Newspapers, Inc. I'. Orange Co1111ty Employees Retirement 
System (1993) 6 Cal.4th 821. 829.) 


Step Three: Is the Mayor making or participating in making a contract? 
File No. C-14-036  
As a member of the Town of Colma City Council, which must approve the contracts with 
PG&E, Mayor Fisicaro would be participating in the making of a contract. 
Step Four: Does the Mayor have a financial interest in the contract? 
Under Section l 090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official 
has a financial interest" (People v. Honig, supra, at p. 333), and officials are deemed to have a 
financial interest in a contract if they might profit from it in any way. (Ibid.) Although Section 
1090 nowhere specifically defines the term "financial interest," case law and Attorney General 
Opinions state that prohibited financial interests may be indirect as well as direct, and may 
involve financial losses, or the possibility of losses, as well as the prospect of pecuniary gain. 
(See e.g., Thomson, supra, at pp. 645, 651-652; see also People v. Vallerga (1977) 67 
Cal.App.3d 847,867, fn. 5.) 
You have presented four possible financial interests, which we discuss in turn. 
First, Mayor Fisicaro has stock holdings in PG&E valued in excess of $2,000 by virtue of 
her 40 l (k) plan. Through that plan, she owns less than three percent of the total shares offered 
by PG&E. Second, Mayor Fisicaro is paid dividends on these stocks, which are automatically 
reinvested into her 40l(k) account. The dividend amounts exceed five percent of the Mayor's 
annual income. You have estimated that one of the contracts between PG&E and the city could 
increase her dividend potential by approximately $0.19 (total). 
Although, as mentioned, Section 1090 does not define the term "financial interest," other 
provisions related to Section 1090 offer guidance on this issue as it relates to stock holdings and 
dividends paid on those stocks. For example, the Legislature has created various statutory 
exceptions to Section 1090's prohibition when the financial interest involved is deemed to be a 
"remote interest," as defined in Section 1091, or a "noninterest," as defined in Section 1091.5. 
Section 109l.5(a)(l) provides that an official is not deemed to be financially interested in a 
contract made with a for-profit corporation when he or she owns less than three percent of the 
shares of the corporation, provided that the total annual income to the official from dividends of 
the stock does not exceed five percent of the official's total annual income and any other 
payments made to the official by the corporation do not exceed five percent of the official's total 
annual income. In addition, Section 1091 (b )(14) provides that an official has a remote interest in 
a public agency's contract with a for-profit corporation when he or she owns less than three 
percent of the shares in the corporation and the stock ownership is derived from the official's 
employment with the corporation. It therefore appears that the Legislature considers stock 
ownership in a corporation with whom a public agency is contracting to be a type of financial 
interest that raises issues under Section l 090. 
Third and fourth, Mayor Fisicaro, as a former employee of PG&E, has an interest in a 
40l(a) defined-benefit pension plan offered by PG&E and, as a retiree, receives payments from 


File No. C-14-036  
that plan. As a defined-benefit plan, the pension benefits are guaranteed regardless of the 
performance of the underlying assets. 
Section 1090 offers no guidance on whether an interest in a defined-benefit pension plan 
and fixed payments under that plan are a type of financial interest. Therefore, we look to other 
sources for assistance. First, in determining a Section 1090 financial interest, courts "generally 
focus on whether the contract in question could confer some type of pecuniary advantage to the 
target of a Section 1090 inquiry." (See Eden Township Healthcare District v. Sutter Health 
(2011) 202 Cal. App. 4th 208, 225.) In Eden Township, the Court concluded that an employee 
who received a salary from a contracting party, but whose salary or benefits would be in no way 
affected by the contract, did not have a financial interest in the contract. (Id. at pp. 221-222 and 
p. 228 ["In our view, if the contract itself offers no benefit to the official, either directly or 
indirectly, then the official is not financially interested in the contract .... "].) Similarly, the 
Mayor's 40l(a) plan and payments made under the plan, which are defined,fixed benefits, will 
not be, and indeed ca1111ot be by operation of law, affected by any contract between PG&E and 
the City of Colma. Based on this analysis, we conclude that, under these facts, the defined 
benefit under the 401(a) plan (and payments made under the plan) is not a financial interest for 
purposes of Section 1090.File No. C-14-036  
As a general rule, when Section 1090 is applicable to one member of a governing body of 
a public entity, as here, the prohibition cannot be avoided by having the interested board member 
abstain; the entire governing body is precluded from entering into the contract. (Thomson, 
supra, at pp. 647-649; Stigall, supra, at p. 569; 86 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 138, 139 (2003); 70 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 45, 48 (1987).) As mentioned, however, the Legislature has created various 
statutory exceptions to Section 1090's prohibition, one of which defines remote interests under 
Section 1091. 
No11-/11terest Exception 
As discussed above, an official is not deemed to be financially interested in a contract 
made with a for-profit corporation when he or she owns less than three percent of the shares of 
the corporation, provided that the total annual income to the official from dividends of the stock 
does not exceed five percent of the official's total annual income and any other payments made 
to the official by the corporation do not exceed five percent of the official's total annual income. 
(Section 1091.S(a)(l).) In Mayor Fisicaro's case, she owns less than three percent of PG&E's 
stock, but receives more than five percent of her total annual income from the dividends. It can 
be argued that, because her stock dividends are not paid directly to her as income but are instead 
used by PG&E to purchase more stock on her behalf, that the dividends are technically not part 
of her annual income, and thus her stock interest (and dividends) are a noninterest under Section 
1091.S(a)(l).4 We do not, however, view the concept of "income" under Section 1090 to rest 
upon technical distinctions, such as, for example, that the payment of these dividends may be 
treated differently under tax law than direct dividend payments made to an official. Here, the 
payments are made to the benefit of Mayor Fisicaro through the plan, and the only difference is 
the form in which she receives them. On this basis, we cannot conclude that Mayor Fisicaro's 
stock ownership in PG&E (and the dividends) are a noninterest under Section 1091.S(a)( l ). 
Remote lllterest Exception 
If a "remote interest" is present, the contract may be made if ( 1) the officer in question 
discloses his or her financial interest in the contract to the public agency, (2) such interest is 
noted in the entity's official records, and (3) the officer abstains from any participation in the 
making of the contract. (Section 1091 (a); 88 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. l 06, 108 (2005); 83 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 246, 248 (2000).) 
As stated above, a remote interest includes "a person who owns less than three percent of 
the shares of a contracting party that is a for•profit corporation, provided that the ownership of 
the shares derived from the person's employment with that corporation." (Section 109l(b)(l 4).) 
The PG&E stock held in Mayor Fisicaro's 40l(k) account fits precisely under this exception. 
Moreover, we presume that the Legislature, in creating this exception, recognized that dividends 
are commonly paid on stock shares held in a corporation and that these were not by themselves a 
separate financial interest for purposes of this exception. Accordingly, we conclude that the 
« If a "noninterest'' is present, the contract may be made without the officer's abstention, and generally a 
noninterest does not require disclosure. (City of Vemo11 \'. Cemraf Basin M1111. Water Dist. ( 1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 
508, 514-515; 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 159-160 (2001 ).) 


File No. C-14-036  
401 (k) account (and dividends paid on the stock in the account) is a remote interest for purposes 
of Section 1090. 
Mayor Fisicaro therefore has a remote interest in her 401(k) plan. The city council may 
enter into the contract with PG&E, but the Mayor may not participate, as explained above.5 
Step Six: Does the rule of necessity apply? 
In limited circumstances, a "rule of necessity" has been applied to allow the making of a 
contract that Section 1090 would otherwise prohibit. (88 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 106, 110 (2005).) 
Under the rule of necessity, a government agency may acquire an essential service, despite the 
existence of a conflict, when no source other than that which triggers the contract is available; 
the rule "ensures that essential government functions are performed even where a conflict of 
interest exists." (Eldridge v. Sierra View Hospital Dist. (1990) 224 Cal. App. 3d 311, 322.) 
You have provided no facts to suggest the "rule of necessity" would apply in the present 
situation.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does Section 1090 prevent the Town of Colma city council from entering into a contract 
with PG&E, a company in which Mayor Fisicaro may have financial interests?

CONCLUSION: No. While Mayor Fisicaro does have a remote interest in PG&E under Section 1090, the 
City Council is not prohibited from entering the contract, provided that Mayor Fisicaro does not 
participate and follows the correct procedures applicable to disqualification when an official has 
a remote interest. 
1 All further statutory references arc to the· Government Code, unless otherwise indicated.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 March 21, 2014 Roger C. Peters BEST BEST & KRIEGER 2001 N Main Street, Suite 390 Walnut Creek, CA 94596 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. C-14-036 Dear Mr. Peters: This letter responds to your request on behalf of Colma Mayor Helen Fisicaro for advice regarding the conflicts-of-interest provisions under Government Code Section 1090 et seq.¹ Because the Fair Political Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), this letter is based on the facts presented. Please note that after forwarding your request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Mateo District Attorney's Office, we did not receive a written response from either entity. (See Section 1097.1(c)(4).) Finally, we are required to advise you that the advice given in this letter is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor. (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).) QUESTION Does Section 1090 prevent the Town of Colma city council from entering into a contract with PG&E, a company in which Mayor Fisicaro may have financial interests? CONCLUSION No. While Mayor Fisicaro does have a remote interest in PG&E under Section 1090, the City Council is not prohibited from entering the contract, provided that Mayor Fisicaro does not participate and follows the correct procedures applicable to disqualification when an official has a remote interest. _______________ ¹ All further statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. File No. C-14-036 Page No. 2 FACTS You are the city attorney for the Town of Colma, a general law city located in San Mateo County and a customer of PG&E for its electric and gas services. Mayor Helen Fisicaro is a retired employee of PG&E as of February 1, 2010. As a former employee, Mayor Fisicaro has a 401(k) plan comprised entirely of PG&E Corporation stock in an amount in excess of $2,000. The Mayor's 401(k) plan holds less than three percent of the outstanding shares of PG&E Corporation common stock and the dividends exceed five percent of her annual income. The dividends paid on the Mayor's stock to her 401(k) plan are not paid to her as income. Rather, they are reinvested into more PG&E Corporation stock in the 401(k) plan. The dividends are not treated as income to her for tax purposes. In addition to the 401(k) plan, the Mayor also receives payments from an Internal Revenue Code Section 401(a) qualified, defined benefit pension plan that was established through her previous employment at PG&E. As a defined benefit plan, the pension benefits are guaranteed regardless of the performance of the underlying assets, and the nature of those underlying assets is immaterial to the Plan's beneficiaries. The City Council of the Town of Colma is contemplating entering into two agreements with PG&E. Mayor Fisicaro intends to disclose her interests in her 401(k)
